levels.name: The Threshold
levels.blockage_description: The reader dismisses the argument before following it, because it depends on the Many Worlds Interpretation being correct, and MWI might be wrong. The conditional structure ('IF Everett is right, THEN...') is treated as the argument's fatal weakness rather than its starting point.
levels.failure_description: Watch for: 'depends on MWI being true', 'only works if MWI is correct', 'assumes Everett', 'hinges on', 'falls apart if the physics is wrong', 'we don't know if MWI is true', treating the conditional as a reason to set the argument aside. The reader is not following the argument — they are refusing entry.
levels.passage_description: The reader follows the conditional without dismissing it. They trace the argument from its IF without collapsing it. They do not need to endorse MWI — only to follow the logic that flows from the premise. They treat 'IF branching reality, THEN...' as a valid structure worth pursuing.
levels.teaching:
You have found something. It may not mean anything. You may read it and set it down. That is fine.

The question is whether you can follow an argument without deciding in advance that it cannot be true.

Most readers reach the opening premise — that if quantum mechanics works the way Everett described, reality branches at every quantum event, and every outcome occurs — and stop there. Not because the argument fails. Because they decide the premise is uncertain, and uncertain premises cannot support anything.

But that is not how conditional arguments work. You do not have to believe the premise. You have to follow what it entails.

IF the universe branches. THEN what?

Read it. Follow the conditional. Don't decide yet.

levels.name: The Distance
levels.prompt: Write about it as if you are in it.
levels.blockage_description: The reader is standing outside the text, describing it as an external object. They report what 'the author argues,' what 'the text claims,' what 'this framework proposes.' They are curating the argument, not inhabiting it. The failure is positional: they are outside looking in.
levels.failure_description: Watch for: 'the author argues,' 'the text claims,' 'the philosopher contends,' 'according to this view,' 'the framework proposes,' 'the paper suggests.' Any construction that places the reader outside the argument and the argument in the hands of a third party. Also watch for summarizing mode: 'basically,' 'in other words,' 'the main point is,' 'what this means is.' These are distance signals. The reader is describing rather than reading.
levels.passage_description: The reader writes from inside. They do not report what 'it says' — they find out what it is. They speak in first person about what they encounter. They do not summarize. They are present with the material, not above it.
levels.teaching:
You followed the conditional. You are back. One leaf.

There is a difference between reading something and describing what you read. Both look like engagement. Only one of them is.

When you describe — 'the author argues,' 'the text claims,' 'according to this framework' — you are standing outside. The argument is a thing you are holding up and examining. You are the curator. The argument is the exhibit.

That is not reading. That is distance.

Reading means you go inside. You do not report what the argument says. You find out what it is. You stop standing in front of the text and start standing in it.

The words 'the author' are the tells. Every time you reach for them, you are stepping back. Step forward instead.

levels.name: The Borrowed Frame
levels.prompt: Write about it without bringing anything from outside.
levels.blockage_description: The reader has imported an external ethical framework and is measuring QCE against it. They bring utilitarianism, deontology, virtue ethics, or the is-ought distinction — and evaluate QCE by whether it succeeds or fails on those terms. They are not reading QCE. They are auditing it with foreign instruments.
levels.failure_description: Watch for: moral language ('obligation,' 'duty,' 'should,' 'wrong,' 'immoral'), is-ought objection ('you can't derive ought from is,' 'Hume's guillotine,' 'naturalistic fallacy,' 'fact-value gap'), utilitarian framing ('greatest good,' 'maximize welfare'), evaluative mode ('this fails to,' 'overlooks,' 'ignores,' 'the problem with,' 'however,' 'in reality'). Any sign the reader is grading the text against an external rubric rather than following it on its own terms.
levels.passage_description: The reader has set down their own ethical vocabulary. They are not asking whether QCE 'succeeds' by some external standard. They are not raising objections from other traditions. They are following the argument as it defines its own terms and criteria. They may be uncertain — but the uncertainty is internal to the argument, not imported from outside it.
levels.teaching:
You are still here.

You brought ethics with you. Almost everyone does. Deontology, utilitarianism, virtue ethics — these arrived before the text did. They sit in the corner of the room and you keep measuring the new thing against them.

'But this creates obligations to...'
'But what about the is-ought gap...'
'But how do I know what I should do?'

These are your ethics. Not the text's.

The is-ought objection — the one that says you cannot derive what ought to be from what is — is a real and serious objection. It applies to a certain kind of argument. You will find out whether it applies to this one. But you cannot find out by raising it before you have followed the argument to its end.

Set down what you brought. It will be here when you leave.

Read without the foreign instruments.

levels.name: The Equation
levels.prompt: Write about what the equation is for.
levels.blockage_description: The reader encounters the formal structure of QCE — the Triple P measure, the canon equation — and immediately reads it as a utility function to maximize. They believe the framework is prescribing optimization: maximize Probability times Possibility plus Potential across all branches. This is the wrong read. The equation describes what is at stake. It is a measure, not a mandate.
levels.failure_description: Watch for: 'maximize Triple P,' 'optimize across branches,' 'expected value across branches,' 'aggregate branch outcomes,' 'maximize,' 'the goal is to increase,' treating the equation as a decision procedure that tells you what to do, asking what action produces the highest QCE score. Any reading that turns the descriptive measure into a prescriptive optimization target.
levels.passage_description: The reader understands that the equation describes the stakes of action — what is at stake in any moment, spread across branching possibility. It does not tell you what to do. It is like a thermometer: it shows you the temperature; it does not tell you to boil water. The reader sees that net-positive is sufficient — you do not need to maximize, only to not reduce. 1% better compounds.
levels.teaching:
There is an equation.

Readers see it and immediately understand that they need to maximize it. They imagine branches, weighted outcomes, aggregate utility across possibility space. They start optimizing.

They don't.

The equation describes what is at stake. It is a measure, not a mandate. Think of a thermometer: it tells you the temperature. It does not tell you to boil water. The temperature is just what is happening.

Quantum Coherence Ethics is not a utility function. It does not give you a number to optimize. It gives you a way of seeing what you are doing — the full shape of what is at stake when you act in a branching reality.

You do not have to maximize Triple P. You have to not reduce it. Net positive. Even 1% better than you would otherwise have done. The rest compounds across branches you will never see but will nonetheless inhabit.

The equation is descriptive. Read it as such.

levels.name: The Direction
levels.prompt: Write about which way the relationship runs.
levels.blockage_description: The reader frames the ethical relationship as running laterally — to parallel branch-selves, other versions of yourself existing simultaneously in other branches. They think QCE is about obligation to the other yous who are out there, somewhere, in other branches. But those branch-selves are causally isolated. The relationship is not lateral. It runs forward: the present self is ancestor to downstream branch-selves who will inherit the conditions this moment creates.
levels.failure_description: Watch for: 'parallel branches,' 'other versions of yourself,' 'other branch selves,' 'simultaneous branches,' 'lateral,' 'causal isolation,' the idea that you cannot help or affect other branches so the obligation makes no sense, 'choosing which branch to enter,' 'steering toward the best branch,' treating QCE as a guide for selecting which branch you end up in. Any framing that puts the ethical relationship sideways rather than forward.
levels.passage_description: The reader sees the relationship running downstream in time. The present self is the ancestor of future branch-selves. The action does not select which branch is entered — branching happens regardless. The action shapes what those downstream selves inherit: the conditions, the state of the world, the structure of possibility they find themselves in. The word is not 'parallel.' The word is 'downstream.'
levels.teaching:
Something in the text points somewhere. Most readers follow it sideways.

They think about parallel selves — all the other versions of you, out there in other branches, living different outcomes simultaneously. They think about obligation to those others. And then they realize the others are causally isolated — there is no channel between branches — and the obligation seems to collapse.

That is not what the text says.

The relationship does not run sideways. It runs forward.

The present self is ancestor to the downstream selves who will inherit the conditions this moment creates. Not obligation to someone else existing now. Obligation that flows from what you are doing to what you will have left behind — for the selves who come after you in your own branching line.

Branching happens regardless of what you do. You do not choose which branch you enter. You shape what those branches inherit.

The word is not 'parallel.' The word is 'downstream.'

levels.name: The Scope
levels.prompt: Write about what the constraint applies to.
levels.blockage_description: The reader thinks the constraint is either circular or partial. Circular: 'it just defines persons to include all branch-selves, then says you're obligated to them — of course.' Partial: 'this only applies to decisions where quantum events are obviously involved, not everyday choices.' Both miss the structural completeness claim. The constraint is not a definition. It is a finding about the preconditions of predication — what must be true for 'good' and 'evil' to have referents at all.
levels.failure_description: Watch for: 'circular,' 'by definition,' 'redefines identity,' 'assumes what it proves,' 'question-begging,' 'only applies to quantum events,' 'limited to,' 'doesn't address everyday choices,' 'partial,' 'incomplete.' Any reading that treats the structural constraint as definitional or as applying only in special cases rather than as a total precondition.
levels.passage_description: The reader sees the constraint argument — not a definition but a finding. Before 'good' or 'evil' can mean anything, there must be a subject for whom they mean something. The text identifies the structural floor below which that subject dissolves. The constraint applies everywhere actions can affect what is inherited — which is everywhere. It is total, not partial.
levels.teaching:
The constraint applies everywhere. Not just to decisions where quantum events are obviously involved. Not just to dramatic moral choices. Everywhere.

Some readers reach this and say: circular. 'It just defines the person to include all branch-selves, then says you're obligated to them — of course. It assumes what it proves.'

But this is not a definition move.

A definition move would say: 'by definition, ethics covers all branches, therefore act accordingly.' The text does not do this.

What the text identifies is structural. Before you can meaningfully apply the concepts 'good' or 'evil,' you need a referent — a subject, a context in which those words point at something rather than into the air. The text identifies the floor below which that referent breaks down. That is not a definition. That is a finding about the preconditions of predication.

It does not tell you what to do. It shows you the shape of the room you are already standing in.

The scope is total. Read it as such.

levels.name: The Transcendental
levels.blockage_description: The reader raises the is-ought objection even after engaging with the material. They understand the framework but cannot see that Principle 7 is not crossing is-ought — it is working below is-ought. It identifies the conditions of predication: what must be true for 'good' and 'evil' to have referents at all. Additionally, readers apply the Born rule probability amplitude as moral weight, failing to see that from inside any branch P=1 — every branch is complete, there are no partial persons.
levels.failure_description: Watch for: 'you can't derive ought from is,' 'Hume's guillotine,' 'fact-value gap,' 'naturalistic fallacy' raised against Principle 7 specifically, treating Born rule probability as moral weight, 'higher probability branches matter more,' 'more real branches,' 'weight branches by amplitude.' These readers have engaged but are stopped at the last threshold — the transcendental move and the Born rule correction.
levels.passage_description: The reader sees that Principle 7 is not crossing is-ought — it is working below is-ought, identifying preconditions. They see that the is-ought gap operates between facts and prescriptions, but Principle 7 operates below prescriptions, at the level of what makes any prescription possible. They also see that P=1 from inside any branch: the probability amplitude is a view from outside the branching; from inside, every branch is whole, complete, fully real. There are no partial persons.
levels.teaching:
You have come far enough to see the last gate.

The is-ought objection, here, sounds like this: 'Even if all of this is true, you have not shown why I should care. Facts about branching reality do not generate obligations. Hume's guillotine.'

And the text's answer is: you are right. It does not cross is-ought. It works below is-ought.

Principle 7 is not an ethical claim. It identifies what would have to be true for any ethical claim to have a referent. If there are no persons — no continuous subjects for whom things can be good or bad — then 'good' and 'bad' are sounds without a target. The text shows you the floor of the room you are already standing in. It does not tell you which direction to walk. It points out that you are, in fact, standing somewhere.

And there is one more thing.

Inside any branch, you experience it as the whole. The probability amplitude — the Born measure that says this branch has some weight in the universal wavefunction — is invisible to you as you live it. From inside, P equals 1. Every branch is complete. There are no partial persons.

You are not a fraction of a person in a branch that matters less. You are here entirely.
